stories.title: The Locksmith's Last Refusal
stories.text:
Gorvin had picked locks for forty years without asking what lay behind them. Tonight, he would break that covenant. The Dominion's auditor pressed a brass key into his palm—meteoric iron core, bone-resonance threaded through the shaft. "Open the deepest vault beneath Keshtir," the woman said. Her shadow moved wrong, as if it belonged to someone else. "Inside: three hundred consciousness-fragments we've seized from refugees. Bring them to the furnace-houses. Glass will burn them to fuel."

Gorvin had opened vaults for emperors and thieves, for merchants and grave-robbers. But he had never opened one knowing the price would be paid in stolen lives. He descended seven floors into stone that still remembered the hammers that shaped it. The lock sang when his picks touched it—an old song, the kind bone-singers taught, a language of tumblers and intention. The vault door swung inward. Three hundred glass vessels, each holding a fragment of someone's joy, memory, terror. Gorvin set down his tools. The auditor's footsteps echoed above, waiting. He took the first vessel and smashed it against the vault's stone wall.

stories.title: The Deepkin Defector's Choice
stories.text:
Krell the Ironbound had sworn an oath to the deepkin Council three hundred years ago. Tonight, she would unmake it. The Tear of the Drowned God had begun to crack. Not suddenly—the fissure had widened for decades as the abyss pushed against the seal, as surface-dwellers spilled refugees into the boundary-waters, as the old covenant between deep and shallow grew thin as moth-wing. The Council demanded the traditional renewal: a willing sacrifice from the surface peoples, drowned in the pearl-chamber to reblind the Tear's weakening eye. Krell had collected such sacrifices before. She had watched surface-dwellers go to their drowning with songs in their throats, their deaths woven into the covenant-pearls that held the boundary. It was ancient law. It was necessary. But yesterday, a bone-singer had come to the boundary-waters and asked: "What if the covenant renewed itself through *choice*, not through death? What if every dawn, the deepkin and the surface chose—deliberately, consciously—to maintain the seal?"

The Council had laughed. Covenants required payment. Payment required sacrifice.

stories.title: The Wyvern-Hoarder's Reckoning
stories.text:
Skarath had collected for two hundred years. Gold, pearls, consciousness-fragments, stolen memories, the signet rings of fallen kings. Her crystalline vault in the Ironspine caverns contained mountains of it all. She had counted and recounted and counted again, each piece of treasure confirming her existence: I am, therefore I hoard. Then the Luminous Ascension rewrote the rules of matter itself. One morning, Skarath discovered that all her gold had become soft—not melted, but *willing* to change shape. The consciousness-fragments in their sealed vessels sang different songs each time she opened them. The stolen memories had begun arguing with each other, creating new narratives that didn't match their original echoes. The treasure she had built her identity upon had become fluid, alive, uncontrollable. In despair, she did something a wyvern never does: she asked a question. A bone-singer had wandered into her cavern by accident, seeking shelter from a blizzard. Instead of fleeing, Skarath asked: "If my hoard is no longer fixed, who am I?"

The bone-singer smiled. "What if you are not your hoard?

stories.title: The Merchant's Double Ledger
stories.text: Isren kept two ledgers: one for the Hegemony, one for the truth. For five years, she had run spice caravans across the Scorched Reach, paying the Sovereignty's tax assessors their cut, recording sales in official ink on Dominion paper. Every transaction accounted for, every coin tracked, every mile of trade measured in the Hegemony's brutal certainty. But beneath the city of Kaethmir, in a hidden scriptorum, she maintained a counter-ledger written in refugee testimonies, in whispered prices, in the real weight of what trade cost those who bore it. The official ledger said the Dominion was prosperous. The shadow-ledger said children starved while spice was burned for dye. It said merchants went mad trying to reconcile the two accounts. It said the Sovereignty's certainty was built on a lie so large that even the Hegemony's own auditors had begun to crack. Then the young auditor arrived—Vel, barely older than the ledgers themselves, assigned to verify Isren's accounts. Most auditors saw numbers. Vel saw contradictions.

stories.title: The Archer's Vigil
stories.text: Ashara had held Thornwick fortress for forty days alone. Now, three winters later, she still climbed its highest tower each dawn to watch the valley below—not for enemies, but for the one thing that had escaped her when the wyvern-siege finally broke. The creature had come from the north, a wound-wing dragon bound by blood-oath to feed on the innocent of Ashenvale village. It had circled Thornwick daily, testing her defenses, daring her to waste arrows. Ashara had never missed a shot—her bow remembered every archer who had held it before her, guiding her arrows true. But thirty-nine days into the siege, the wyvern had descended on the village itself, and Ashara had made a choice: she fired not at the creature's heart, but through the oath-sigil burned into its scales. The arrow had shattered the binding, not the beast. The wyvern had shrieked and fled north into the unmapped mountains, freed from compulsion but leaving behind the weight of centuries of forced killing. Ashara had named it Valorix and never seen it again.

stories.title: The Lockpick's Confession
stories.text:
Gorven the Conjurer had spent forty years opening locks that should never be opened. Tonight, he would close one forever. The Dominion's vault lay seven floors beneath Keshtir's administrative heart, sealed by three master-locks of meteoric iron and bone-resonance. Gorven's hands trembled as he approached the innermost chamber. Inside waited what the auditors called the Sovereign's Collection: consciousness-fragments harvested from refugees, each one a stolen memory, a shattered life. He had forged the locks that kept them. He had sung the bone-resonance into the iron himself. Mara, his junior auditor daughter—the one he'd sworn never to see again after the Dominion took her as collateral for his silence. "The ledgers you smuggled to the Counter-Archive," Gorven said. "They showed me what I'd built. Whose suffering paid for my reputation."

Mara held a vial of bioluminescent ink. "The Archive sends word: if we burn the records, the fragments lose their binding. They scatter. The Dominion loses its leverage."

Gorven drew his grandmother's lockpicks—the ones he'd hidden for sixty years—and knelt before the final seal.

stories.title: The Drowned Compass
stories.text:
Captain Nerida had drowned once. The merfolk crown had raised her back, and for seventeen years she served as their emissary to the surface kingdoms, carrying treaties in bone-sealed vessels and prophecies written in bioluminescent ink. Now the Keeper of Pearls demanded she choose: renew the ancient sacrifice, or let the abyss-boundary crumble. "There is a third path," said Korven the Copper-Handed, appearing at the tidemark where Nerida kept her council. The old thief had stolen a compass from a wyvern's hoard—one that did not point north, but toward covenant. Nerida studied the artifact. Swan-bone set in starlight-glass, inscribed with syllables in the Elder Tongue. "This won't hold the boundary," she said. "No. But it might teach the deepkin another way to renew it."

They descended into the Sapphire Deep where the Tear of the Drowned God hung like a scar above the abyss. The Keeper of Pearls rose—ancient, terrible, adorned in pearls harvested from centuries of willing sacrifice. Nerida held up the compass. "Each moon, five surface-dwellers and five deepkin meet at the boundary-waters. Not to bleed.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Ledger
stories.text:
Skarath the Fragment-Hoarder had forgotten why she collected. Three centuries of gathering consciousness-shards, and no memory remained of the first hoard—the one that had made her powerful. She lay in her cavern beneath the Ironspine Mountains, wings folded, surrounded by glass vessels containing the stolen intimacies of a thousand broken lives. The sight no longer moved her. Gold had become weight. Fragments had become noise. A young thief named Kess climbed to her lair with no weapon but a ledger. "I've come to steal from you," Kess announced, setting the ledger on Skarath's treasure-mound. "Not shards or gold. Your story."

Skarath's scales rippled with something like laughter. "My story is a tomb. What use is it?"

"The opposite," Kess said. "The Dominion erases the stories of the people whose consciousness fills those vessels. They become inventory. But if I record why you began hoarding—the wound that made you collect—then those stolen fragments mean something again. They're not currency.

stories.title: The Archer's Last Stand
stories.text:
Ashara had held Thornwick fortress alone for forty days. The wyvern encircling it had not attacked—not yet. It waited, patient as stone, for hunger to break her will. Her bow was Grandmother's Bow, carved from a tree that had grown through a battlefield and remembered every arrow loosed there. It chose its shots with uncanny precision, but even legend had limits. On the forty-first dawn, a voice rose from the mist below. Not the wyvern's roar, but human speech: a woman's voice, calm and deliberate. "The fortress will fall," the woman called. "Your supplies are gone. The wyvern will breach at dusk. Come down, and I will grant you mercy."

Ashara nocked an arrow—not at the woman, but at the mist itself. Grandmother's Bow understood. It released, and the arrow split the fog like a blade, revealing the woman beneath: tall, scarred, bearing a ledger inscribed with names in blood-ink. "I know you," Ashara said. The woman was Kessara, Warlord of the Crimson Vale, who had conquered three provinces through fear and false gold.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Bargain
stories.text:
Valren had been training since childhood to become a bone-singer, inheriting the old craft from the Elder Bone-Singers of the Hearthlands. Tonight, at the threshold of initiation, she would learn whether she had the strength to speak the Elder Tongue without breaking. The First Covenant required her to carve a binding-promise into living bone—her own—and sing it into the pre-human frequencies that still hummed beneath the world. If she succeeded, she would join the Bone-Singers' Collective. If she failed, the magic would consume her sanity, leaving her a hollow vessel. Naela, her grandmother and keeper of the tradition, brought her to the Hidden Catacomb north of Pelimor. There, beneath singing stone walls that had vibrated for ten thousand years, Valren knelt. "What is your promise?" Naela asked. Valren had prepared for this moment her entire life. The answer should have been: *I will preserve the Elder Tongue. I will protect the refugees. I will serve the Bone-Singers' will.*

Instead, she spoke truth. "I promise to question. To refuse what breaks others.
